steps.title: How Trust Actually Works
steps.body:
Trust isn't a switch you flip. It's a muscle you build through repetition.

Most people think trust is rebuilt through grand gestures — the trip, the declaration, the dramatic change. But that's not how it works. Research on trust in relationships shows something more ordinary and more powerful:

Trust is built in small moments. The moment you said you'd text and you did. The moment they were upset and you stayed present instead of shutting down. The moment you chose honesty when a comfortable lie would have been easier.

Those moments, repeated over weeks and months, are what trust is actually made of.

Gottman describes trust as built through what he calls 'sliding door moments' — the countless small choices every day where you either turn toward your partner or away from them.

These moments are easily missed. You walk past someone who's quiet and wondering if you'll ask. You see the dishes and wonder if they'll bother. You have something to say and wonder if it's safe.

In each of these moments, a deposit or a withdrawal is made.

Trust isn't built in crises. It's built in Tuesdays.

steps.title: The ATTUNE Model
steps.body:
Gottman's research on trust-building produced the ATTUNE framework — six behaviors that, when practiced consistently, build the experience of being known and safe.

A — Awareness: Noticing your partner's emotional state. 'You seem off today.'

T — Turning toward: Choosing to engage rather than avoid when they need connection.

T — Tolerance: Making room for their experience even when it differs from yours.

U — Understanding: Trying to genuinely comprehend their world, not just your version of it.

N — Non-defensive: Receiving feedback without counter-attacking or shutting down.

E — Empathy: Feeling with them — not just understanding intellectually, but being moved by what moves them.

ATTUNE isn't a formula. It's a daily orientation.

steps.question: Your partner comes home frustrated after a hard day at work and mentions it briefly, then goes quiet. An ATTUNE response would be:
options: Give them space and let them come to you when they're ready
Share about your own difficult day so they know they're not alone
Notice their state, turn toward them, and ask: 'Do you want to talk about it or just decompress together?'
Change the subject to something lighter to help them shift their mood
feedback: Space has its place, but withdrawing without checking in can feel like indifference. The ATTUNE approach involves turning toward and asking what they need — which might be space, or might be connection.
Sharing your own difficulty can feel like one-upping. The first move in ATTUNE is awareness and turning toward — focusing on them before bringing in your experience.
Exactly. This demonstrates Awareness (noticing), Turning toward (engaging), and Understanding (asking what kind of support they need rather than assuming). It invites without demanding.
Redirecting to lightness can work sometimes, but it often communicates 'your feelings are inconvenient.' First check in — then they can choose how to proceed.

steps.question: Trust in a relationship is most effectively rebuilt through large, significant gestures that demonstrate commitment.
feedback: Correct — research consistently shows that trust is rebuilt through consistent small deposits over time, not grand gestures. Grand gestures can signal intention, but they don't build trust on their own. What builds it is the daily accumulation of small moments where you showed up as someone reliable.

Actually, the research points the opposite direction. Grand gestures can signal intent but don't build trust. Trust is rebuilt through the repeated, consistent experience of small deposits — being present, keeping small commitments, turning toward in daily moments.

steps.title: Trust Deposits in the Wild
steps.body:
David and Amara have been working on their relationship after a period of serious distance. David wants to show he's committed to change.

His instinct: plan a weekend trip, write a long letter, make a big declaration.

What the research says will actually work: Tell her you'll be home for dinner and be home. When she mentions something that matters to her, remember it and bring it up later. When she's upset, stay in the room instead of going quiet. Say what you mean and mean what you say — consistently, in small things.

The trip is nice. The consistency is what changes the nervous system.

Trust is a body experience, not a mind experience. It accumulates through patterns, not moments.

steps.question: Empathy in the ATTUNE model means agreeing with your partner's perspective.
feedback: Correct — empathy means feeling with someone, not agreeing with them. You can fully feel your partner's frustration and still see things differently. Empathy is about emotional presence and understanding, not intellectual agreement.

Not quite. Empathy is about being moved by what moves them — feeling with them emotionally — without needing to agree with their interpretation of events. You can be empathetic and still have a different perspective.

steps.title: The Non-Defensive Challenge
steps.body:
Of the six ATTUNE elements, Non-defensive is usually the hardest.

When someone we love says something critical — or something we interpret as critical — our nervous system reads it as a threat. Defensiveness is the automatic response: push back, deflect, counter-attack.

Non-defensive listening doesn't mean accepting unfair criticism without comment. It means your first move is understanding before defending.

'Let me make sure I understand. You're saying [X]?'

Then — after you've actually heard them — you can share your perspective.

The sequence matters enormously. Understanding first, then responding. Not simultaneously.

steps.question: In the ATTUNE model, the 'N' stands for ___, which means receiving feedback without counter-attacking.
Which of the following best represents what 'Understanding' means in the ATTUNE model?
options: Agreeing with your partner's point of view
Asking enough questions to poke holes in their argument
Genuinely trying to comprehend their experience from the inside
Explaining why they feel the way they do
feedback: Understanding doesn't require agreement. You can fully understand someone's experience and still see things differently.
Questioning to find inconsistencies is more like debate than understanding. The goal is to comprehend, not to counter.

Correct. Understanding means trying to see and feel your partner's world from their vantage point — not evaluating whether they're right, not analyzing their psychology, just genuinely trying to get it.
Explaining why someone feels a way can feel dismissive — it positions you as the expert on their inner life. Understanding is listening, not interpreting.

steps.title: Your ATTUNE Profile
steps.prompt:
Looking honestly at the six elements of ATTUNE — Awareness, Turning toward, Tolerance, Understanding, Non-defensive, Empathy — which are your strengths and which are your growing edges?

For the one that feels hardest: what typically happens in you when you try to practice it? What does it take from you? What do you think it would give your relationship if you practiced it more consistently?

fields.label: One specific trust deposit you will make today
fields.placeholder: e.g. When she tells me about her day, I'll put my phone face-down and ask one follow-up question

fields.label: Which ATTUNE element does this practice most?
How will you remember to do it? (Be specific)
fields.placeholder: e.g. I'll set a reminder at 6pm — the time they usually get home

steps.question: How would you describe the current trust level in your relationship?
options: Strong — I feel very safe and relied upon
Mostly good — with a few specific areas that still feel tender
Fragile — we're rebuilding after some damage
Low — there's significant repair work needed here

steps.title: The Small Moment You Remember
steps.prompt:
Think of a small moment — not a grand gesture — where your partner built trust with you. A moment where they chose you when they didn't have to. Where they stayed when they could have gone quiet.

What was it? Why did it matter? Do they know it mattered?

And: is there a small trust deposit you've been meaning to make that you keep putting off?

steps.title: Day 19 Complete
steps.body:
Trust is built in Tuesdays. Today is your Tuesday.

Make the deposit you planned. Notice what happens.
